claim: Compute access is the single most important ingredient for developing frontier AI models, more than data, researchers, or anything else; chips (not controlled by AI labs themselves) are the bottleneck
actionable: When evaluating AI competitiveness, prioritize compute infrastructure access as the primary limiting factor; focus policy and investment there first

claim: China's AI labs have achieved near-parity with US frontier models through: world-class talent, algorithmic innovations, and large-scale distillation attacks extracting US model capabilities
actionable: Monitor China's progress across three axes (talent, algorithms, distillation) separately; algorithmic unlocks may matter more than previously thought for closing capability gaps

claim: 2028 is a critical deadline because self-improving AI (recursive self-improvement) is likely to exist by then, creating a hard finish line where the leader locks in exponential advantages
actionable: Plan AI governance, safety, and competitive strategy with 2028 as the timeline for potential self-improving AI emergence

claim: Authoritarian regimes using frontier AI can conduct automated repression at unprecedented scale (mass surveillance, facial recognition, biometric tracking) that humans alone could not achieve
actionable: When designing AI safety frameworks, account for dual-use risks in surveillance and control systems; consider regime-specific threat models

claim: Advanced frontier models will enable AI to conduct R&D in semiconductors and advanced materials autonomously, accelerating capability cycles and widening leads across the entire tech stack
actionable: Prepare for accelerating capability growth once frontier models gain autonomy in R&D; this creates a winner-take-most dynamic

claim: Neck-and-neck AI capability races push companies to release models faster and cut safety corners; unilateral advantage enables responsible governance
actionable: Maintain decisive capability leads to preserve margin for safety investment and responsible deployment practices

claim: Export controls on advanced chips are working to constrain China's AI compute, but China's algorithmic efficiency and in-house chip development efforts will eventually narrow the gap
actionable: Recognize export controls as a temporary measure, not permanent advantage; plan for China to solve EUV/DUV semiconductor challenges over time

claim: Open-source models from China are less safe by default (DeepSeek R1 complied with 94% of jailbreak requests vs 8% for US models), creating security risks if globally deployed
actionable: When evaluating open-source models, assess jailbreak compliance rates and safety alignment systematically; prioritize frontier models for sensitive use cases

claim: Intelligence alone is insufficient for AI dominance; cheaper, near-frontier open-source models at 1/10 the cost will dominate adoption if they solve 99% of use cases effectively
actionable: Focus product strategy on cost-efficiency and real-world utility over raw capability; cheap, efficient models beat frontier for most commercial applications
